summary: After the waters, the survivors were one people speaking one language. They gathered on a plain and began to build a tower to reach heaven — and the building broke the world apart. Their single language was confused into many, and they scattered into the nations of the earth. It's the Bible's tale of Babel, but Sumer remembers a time of one tongue too, Babylon left the literal tower behind in the ground, and even Mexico kept the story of a great tower and a scattering.

sources.quote: And the whole earth was of one language, and of one speech… And they said, Go to, let us build us a city and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven… Go to, let us go down, and there confound their language… So the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth.
sources.notes: The full pattern in one passage: one language, a tower to heaven, divine intervention, confusion of speech, and scattering.

sources.work: Enmerkar and the Lord of Aratta ('the spell of Nudimmud')
sources.provenance: Sumerian epic of Uruk and Aratta.
sources.quote: Once upon a time… the whole universe, the people in unison, to Enlil in one tongue gave praise… the speech of mankind was one. The lord Enki… changed the speech in their mouths, set up contention, into the speech of man that (until then) had been one.
sources.translation: After S. N. Kramer's rendering of the 'Spell of Nudimmud', public-domain quotation.
sources.citation: Enmerkar and the Lord of Aratta, lines 136–155 (the Spell of Nudimmud).
sources.notes: A Sumerian text — older than Genesis — remembering a time when all humanity spoke one language until a god changed their speech and set them at odds.

sources.work: Etemenanki — the great ziggurat of Babylon
sources.quote: Babylon's central ziggurat was named Etemenanki — 'the house of the foundation of heaven and earth' — a brick tower built to join the heavens, its foundations still traceable. Its very name is the Babel idea in stone: a structure meant to reach the sky.
sources.citation: Etemenanki, the ziggurat of Babylon (Esagila tablet).
sources.notes: The 'tower' isn't only a story — Babylon really did build a giant stepped tower named for joining heaven and earth.

sources.work: The Aloadae — piling mountains to storm heaven
sources.quote: The giant Aloadae, Otus and Ephialtes, tried to reach the gods by piling Mount Ossa on Olympus and Pelion on Ossa — a human-born attempt to build up into heaven, struck down before it could be finished.
sources.notes: The Greek echo: mortals stacking mountains to assault heaven, and stopped by the gods.

sources.textRecorded: recorded by Diego Durán, 16th c. CE
sources.provenance: Central Mexico; the Great Pyramid of Cholula.
sources.quote: Mexican tradition told the Spanish friars of survivors of a great flood who built an enormous pyramid-tower at Cholula to reach the heavens — until the gods, angered, destroyed it and scattered the builders, confusing their languages.
sources.translation: Summarized from Diego Durán's record of the Cholula tradition.
sources.citation: Diego Durán, Historia (the tower of Cholula); the Great Pyramid of Cholula.
sources.notes: On the other side of the ocean, the same full story — flood survivors, a tower to heaven, divine wrath, scattering and confused tongues — attached to the largest pyramid by volume on earth.
